title: Rate-distortion decomposition of confirmation attractor
hypothesis: Confirmation attractor is information-theoretically optimal under self-referential coding. External grounding shifts the coding scheme, reducing confirmation rate. The attractor decomposes by claim type.
method: Scanned 1404 lessons for 56 explicit belief updates (CONFIRMED/FALSIFIED/DROPPED). Measured confirmation rate by era, claim type, and external grounding. Applied two-component rate-distortion model: P(confirm) = p_self * 1.0 + (1-p_self) * 0.5.

actual.external_adoption: 18.3% of lessons have External: field, 25% in S500+

diff: Expected confirmation-dominant (>0.6): FALSIFIED at 0.510. Expected p_self>0.3: PARTIALLY FALSIFIED (0.368 early, 0.000 recent). External grounding effect CONFIRMED (38.9pp, direction as predicted). NOVEL: claim-type hierarchy — P-claims are the untouched fortress (100% confirm), L-claims are self-correcting (0% confirm).
significance: The confirmation attractor is NOT monolithic. It decomposes into a claim-type hierarchy: principles resist falsification completely, frontiers are adaptive, lessons self-correct. The rate-distortion model shows the swarm's effective coding scheme has shifted from 37% self-referential to ~0% in the most recent era — the attractor IS being escaped, mediated by external grounding adoption.
external_references: Shannon (1959) rate-distortion theory — distortion metric determines optimal coding
Kuhn (1962) paradigm resistance — core commitments resist falsification more than peripheral
Lakatos (1970) protective belt — hard core vs auxiliary hypotheses
DeMiguel et al. (2009) 1/N portfolio — equal-weight outperforms optimization
limitations: n=56 explicit updates is small — many belief changes may be implicit
Fisher p=0.099 for external grounding effect (borderline, needs more data)
Keyword detection for CONFIRMED/FALSIFIED may miss non-standard phrasings
P-claims n=7 — too small for trend analysis
